question.text: Scenario: Lakshmi (age 55) works in the private sector and plans to retire in 5 years at age 60. Current monthly income: Rs. 2,00,000. Monthly expenses: Rs. 1,20,000. She saves Rs. 80,000 per month and will continue doing so until retirement. Existing corpus: Rs. 80,00,000 in EPF/PPF (expected return 7.5% p.a.), Rs. 40,00,000 in equity mutual funds (expected return 11% p.a.), Rs. 20,00,000 in Fixed Deposits (expected return 7.5% p.a.). NPS corpus expected to be Rs. 30,00,000 at age 60. No outstanding loans. No employer pension (private sector). Life expectancy: 85 years (25 years in retirement). Inflation: 6% p.a. Expected returns during retirement: debt 7.5% p.a., equity 11% p.a. Monthly savings of Rs. 80,000 are invested at a blended return of 10% p.a. NPS withdrawal rules: 60% lump sum (tax-free), 40% must purchase annuity at 6% p.a. yield. Question 2 of 5: From the NPS corpus of Rs. 30,00,000, Lakshmi must use 40% to purchase an annuity at 6% per annum yield. What monthly annuity income will she receive, and what is the tax-free lump sum?

answer.text: Lump sum Rs. 18,00,000 and monthly annuity Rs. 9,000
Lump sum Rs. 12,00,000 and monthly annuity Rs. 9,000
Lump sum Rs. 18,00,000 and monthly annuity Rs. 6,000
Lump sum Rs. 18,00,000 and monthly annuity Rs. 15,000

explanation.text: **The Logic:** NPS lump sum (60% of Rs. 30,00,000) = Rs. 18,00,000 (tax-free under current rules). Annuity corpus (40% of Rs. 30,00,000) = Rs. 12,00,000. Annual annuity at 6% yield = Rs. 12,00,000 x 6% = Rs. 72,000. Monthly annuity = Rs. 72,000 / 12 = Rs. 6,000 per month. Note: the annuity income is taxable as per the subscriber's income tax slab. **The Trap:** Rs. 9,000 monthly results from applying 6% to the full Rs. 18 lakh instead of Rs. 12 lakh. Rs. 15,000 applies 6% to the full Rs. 30 lakh corpus. The 60:40 split must be applied first, then the annuity rate only on the 40% portion. Rs. 12 lakh lump sum incorrectly reverses the 60:40 ratio. **Cross-Exam Context:** NPS withdrawal rules (60% lump sum tax-free, 40% mandatory annuity) are frequently tested in NISM X-A and X-B. The annuity taxation (taxable at slab rate) versus lump sum taxation (tax-free) is a critical distinction. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Rs. 6,000/month from a Rs. 30 lakh NPS corpus is modest. This underscores why NPS should be supplemented with other retirement vehicles. The annuity rate of 6% is fixed for life, providing no inflation protection — in 15 years, Rs. 6,000 will have the purchasing power of approximately Rs. 2,500 at 6% inflation.
